title: Quiz: Practical Quantum Monte Carlo
description: Test your understanding of practical QMC implementation strategies for near-term quantum devices

questions.question: What is the main limitation of NISQ devices for quantum amplitude estimation?

options.text: Limited coherence time restricts circuit depth
They only work with classical algorithms
They are too slow for any practical use

questions.explanation: NISQ devices have limited coherence times (50-100 microseconds) and high gate error rates (0.5-1%), which restricts the useful circuit depth to roughly 50-100 gates. Full QAE requires much deeper circuits.

questions.question: What is the purpose of zero-noise extrapolation?

options.text: To run circuits without any noise
To estimate what results would be at zero noise by measuring at multiple noise levels
To remove all errors from measurements

questions.explanation: Zero-noise extrapolation runs the same circuit at artificially increased noise levels, then extrapolates the results backward to estimate what the zero-noise result would be. It's an error mitigation technique, not error elimination.

questions.question: In hybrid classical-quantum Monte Carlo, what is typically done classically?

options.text: All computations, quantum is not used

questions.explanation: Hybrid approaches use classical computers for preprocessing (identifying important regions, dimensionality reduction) and postprocessing (maximum likelihood estimation, bias correction), while quantum handles the core sampling/estimation task.

questions.question: What is the main tradeoff of variational quantum Monte Carlo?

options.text: It uses more qubits but is faster
It has shallow circuits but may not achieve theoretical speedup
It is exact but requires more classical computation
It only works for simple problems

questions.explanation: Variational methods use shallow, trainable circuits that can run on NISQ devices, but they may not achieve the proven quadratic speedup of full QAE. They trade provable speedup for practical executability.

questions.question: Measurement error mitigation requires calibrating the confusion matrix by measuring all 2^n basis states.
questions.explanation: True. Full measurement error mitigation requires characterizing how each basis state is misread as other states, which means preparing and measuring all 2^n computational basis states. This is only practical for small numbers of qubits.

questions.question: How many useful gates can typically be executed on current NISQ devices before errors dominate?
questions.explanation: With 0.5-1% two-qubit gate errors and 50-100 microsecond coherence times, NISQ devices can execute roughly 50-100 gates before errors accumulate to unacceptable levels (below ~50% fidelity).

questions.question: What is the overhead of zero-noise extrapolation error mitigation?
questions.explanation: ZNE requires running circuits at 3-5 different noise levels (original, 1.5x, 2x, etc.) and then extrapolating, resulting in 2-5x overhead in circuit executions compared to a single run.

questions.question: Which are valid circuit optimization strategies for NISQ devices? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Gate synthesis using native gate sets

questions.explanation: Valid strategies include: (a) efficient gate decomposition, (b) minimizing SWAP gates through smart routing, and (c) reformulating problems for shallower circuits. Running unlimited depth (d) is not possible on NISQ devices.
